title: Advanced Border Coordination Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Advanced Border Coordination Act of 2025 requires the Department of Homeland Security to build at least two special centers along the border between the United States and Mexico. These centers will help different law enforcement groups work together better, including federal agents, state police, local police, and tribal officers. The centers will help coordinate efforts to stop illegal activities like drug smuggling, human trafficking, and people crossing the border illegally. The Department of Homeland Security must set up these centers within six months of the bill becoming law.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Border Security and Enforcement.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Advanced Border Coordination Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Establishment of Joint Operations Centers
sections.text:
The Department of Homeland Security must create at least two Joint Operations Centers along the southern border of the United States within six months of this law passing. These centers will be places where law enforcement from federal, state, local, and tribal agencies can work together.

The Centers will serve as central command hubs for several important tasks. They will help coordinate and communicate between different agencies during field operations. They will coordinate operations across all participating agencies, including activities on land, in the air, and on water. The Centers will also coordinate and support border operations to stop and catch criminal activity. This includes fighting transnational criminal organizations (criminal groups that operate across country borders), stopping illegal border crossings, seizing weapons, seizing drugs, seizing valuable stolen property, preventing terrorism, stopping human trafficking, and stopping drug trafficking. The Secretary of Homeland Security can add other activities as needed.

To make sure information flows properly between all participating agencies, the Centers must share important information from federal agencies with state, local, and tribal agencies. Federal agencies must also tell state, local, and tribal agencies about operations happening in their areas.

The Centers must keep track of where personnel are deployed and coordinate their training when needed.

Starting one year after this law passes and every year after that, the Secretary of Homeland Security must write a report to Congress. The Secretary must talk with participating federal agencies and get feedback from state, local, and tribal agencies for this report. The report must describe efforts to establish the Centers, identify what resources are used to operate the Centers, describe the key operations each Center coordinated and supported, describe any major communication problems between agencies at each Center, recommend ways to improve coordination and communication between federal agencies for current and future Centers, and include other information the Secretary thinks is appropriate.

In this section, Centers means the Joint Operations Centers created under this law. Department means the Department of Homeland Security. Participating federal agency means the Department of Homeland Security, the Department of Defense, the Department of Justice, and any other federal agency the Secretary thinks is appropriate. Secretary means the Secretary of Homeland Security. State means each state of the United States, the District of Columbia, and any territory or possession of the United States.
